data.title: International Flavors & Fragrances Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by International Flavors & Fragrances Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: U.S.-China Trade Drops Ahead Of Trump's 'Liberation Day' Tariffs
data.description:
The combined value of goods exchanged between the U.S. and China was $40.8 billion in March 2025. That is the lowest figure recorded by the U.S. Census Bureau outside of 2020's Coronavirus Pandemic since March 2016.

The volume of trade has dropped off significantly since January 2025, which marked the end of a period of frontrunning (or frontloading) imports and exports between the two nations. The timing of the end of the frontrunning of goods coincides with two major factors. First, several trade restrictions and higher tariffs imposed by the Biden administration went into effect in January 2025, extending its worse than the first Trump administration's track record. Second, the incoming Trump administration had signaled it would take a more aggressive approach to trade with China and also with the rest of the world.

President Trump rolled out those measures in the form of elevated tariffs after markets closed on 2 April 2025, which he branded as "Liberation Day". The March 2025 trade data marks the end of a period in which those new, higher tariffs did not exist.

To measure their impact going forward, we need to create a counterfactual trend, which would represent how trade between the U.S. and China would have grown in their absence. For that, we've developed a linear regression based on the trailing 12-month average of the combined value of goods traded between the U.S. and China from March 2024 through March 2025. The trailing 12-month average data lets us smooth out much of the seasonal variation in this data to better track overall trends over time.

We've presented that new counterfactual of what the monthly level of trade between the U.S. and China would look like in the absence of the global tariff war in the following chart, projecting it through January 2029.

This method of projecting a counterfactual is different from the counterfactuals that we've previously presented in this ongoing series, which were based on how trade recovered between the two nations after the 2008-09 Great Recession. Here, because we're not dealing with a recoveries in trade after it bottomed, we're instead taking advantage of a different pattern that prevails in historic data. The level of trade between the U.S. and China tends to follow a linear trajectory outside of periods of transition, such as the onset of recessions and recoveries, and other events such as tariff wars and pandemics.

Given the magnitude of the announced U.S. tariffs and China's retaliatory tariffs, we expect a dramatic departure when we first present the trade data for April 2025 after it becomes available in June 2025.

References

U.S. Census Bureau. U.S. International Trade in Goods and Services (FT900). U.S. Trade in Goods with China, Not Seasonally Adjusted, Nominal Figures, Total Census Basis. [Online database]. Accessed 6 May 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: NuScale Power: A Voting Machine Based On Speculations Rather Than Value
data.description:
Since NuScale Power (NYSE:SMR) went public on May 2, 2022, the company is up by around 70.00%. Through this time though, they have experienced a great deal of ups and downs, first falling by around 80.00% to their all-time lows of around $2.00, to then skyrocket by about 1500.00% to their all-time highs, after the market started believing that small modular reactors (SMRs) would be the solution to providing energy for AI data centers.

Since its all-time highs, the company's stock price is down by 47.46%, after the hype surrounding AI has fallen. This fall was further pushed by the US's ChatGPT rival DeepSeek, stating that building their LLM cost 90 - 95% less than building other commercialized LLMs, undermining investors' beliefs regarding high power demand estimates for AI.

Today, I will be rating SMR as a SELL, mainly due to the company's lasting unprofitability and questionable future regarding the superior position in the production of greener energy.

Company Overview

We are currently heading to SMR's Q1'25 earnings, which I don't think too highly of after the company's last report. Reporting their Q4'24 as well as FY24 earnings in March, the company announced high revenue of $34.20M, but the net income sat at -$137.00M, the lowest it has been through the past 5Y, not managing to break the company's negative 5Y Net income CAGR of 21.60%.

Seeing the graph above, despite the company's revenue growing at a 5Y CAGR of 169.00%, it can be deduced that indeed SMR isn't at all increasing their value, confirming that the stock price surge last year was indeed irrational and investments made in the company are currently on the speculative level.

Through the short term, there isn't much light

Before highlighting my long-term view and stating my worries, let's take a look at the shorter term, specifically at the insider trading and the analysts' expectations.

Through the next 5Y, SMR is expected to stay in the negative, with EPS turning to positive only after 2030, as predicted by SA analysts'.

This means that very probably, the company won't start returning any real value through the shorter term, making them a volatile voting machine based mainly on news.

The positive side to SMR's story, though, is one that creates a somewhat tangible base, and one of the reasons why the company has surged so much through the past year is the fact that some of the world's largest companies are investing into small modular reactors. For example, Google (GOOGL) is partnering with Kairos Power to develop a number of reactors and have them operational by 2030, while Amazon (AMZN) has made an investment in X-energy, helping to finance the construction of SMRs.

This news is something the future growth of the company can actually be based on, as it provides proof that data centers might actually at some point in the future be powered by SMR's.

Turning back to the bear case though, through the past year, insiders have been net sellers of the company's stock, as you can see on the graph below. To me, this shows that management doesn't believe in the company's near-term future, or they trust that it has become overvalued and are predicting a further stock price decline.

Furthermore, looking at SMR's shares outstanding, they have grown from 51.00M in 2022 to 93.00M in 2024, increasing at a CAGR of 23.51%. This again, to me, isn't a great sign, as it means that the company has been losing value, diluting the market, and lowering their future EPS.

Long-term perspective

As much as I believe in nuclear energy and nuclear reactors, looking at the long term from a realistic angle, solar energy might be more superior both from the capacity and the cost perspective.

For the past few years, solar panel prices have been decreasing, meaning that in the future, solar might become much more cost-effective than any other energy-generating source.

The issue with solar, though, and the reason why many companies as well as states are turning to nuclear power is the fact that to produce high amounts of power, solar panels take up a huge area, which many entities can't afford, or can, but use in different, more profitable ways, turning to nuclear power, as that, although requiring high maintenance costs as well as build costs, produces much more power per meter squared.

Nevertheless, below you can see that through 2050, the percentage share of nuclear power produced in the US is expected to decrease from around 19.00% to only 12.00%, while renewable energy is expected to more than double, with solar leading the charge.

This of course are the predictions for the whole of US including commercial and household energy consumption, and therefore, might not be too accurate for the future use of SMRs.

It is though something I want to point out, because even though being really obvious, the cost of producing energy is a great deal, and with SMR modules being extremely expensive to build, not talking about the operating costs, many companies might choose to go with the solar option.

So far, what I have been trying to say is that both the short term, and the long term future for this company are greatly unpredictable, and with their value decreasing with time due to large share sellouts and unprofitability, coupled with their huge "irrational" price surge from 2.00 to their current price, I believe that the company could see a large stock pre decline at any moment, with any given bad news.

Valuation

Nevertheless, let's try to value SMR, with the sentiment that indeed in the future small modular reactors will be the go-to energy source for larger companies as well as smaller states.

Taking the company's predicted EPS of $0.31 in 2030, I will back track to the realistic positive value the company could have now, using an EPS CAGR of 73.33%. This will give me an EPS value of around $0.04, meaning that if indeed in 2030 the company's EPS was to be $0.31, at a future CAGR of 73.33%, one share should now be worth $3.76, which is 78.02% lower than the current trading price.

For the second valuation model, the DCF model, we will use the same backtracking method, but with the FCF. This will give us a realistic FCF value of $3.00M, to which when we apply a future 10Y+ CAGR of 40.00%, we will get an intrinsic value of $6.29 after using a discount rate of 10.00% is applied, accounting for a larger safety margin.

Averaging out the two values, we will get an intrinsic value of $4.52, 70.62% lower than the current trading price of $17.11, supporting my SELL rating.

I do want to clarify that the back tracking method through which I calculated the intrinsic value is mainly based on the predicted FCF and EPS of the company, which might at all not end up to be true, so do take the values with a decently sized pinch of salt.

Conclusion

Nevertheless, in conclusion I do have to rate NuScale Power (NYSE:SMR) as a SELL. The reason why this rating doesn't stand as a strong sell, despite the apparent overvaluation is because of the company's potential to do really well through the next decades, despite currently being an investment full of risk, with an unpredictable future.

SMR's current stock price is heavily effected by news and speculations rather than actual value, making it really volatile, and in my eyes heavily overvalued and even though they are growing their revenue really well, their profitability is in the question due to the high cost of SMRs, again, supporting the thought that possibly, solar energy might become the go-to energy source despite its lower energy generating capabilities.

With that, through the next years I do not expect SMR to turn profitable, unless of course there is some spectacular change in sentiment. Therefore, I would for now certainly stay away from this company because as I already said, they are a voting machine, with their stock price based on news rather than real value, and so there is nothing one can base their long term investment on.

data.title: The Power Of Tax Deferral
data.description:
By Jacob Greene, Head Strategist, NB Custom Direct Investing

Postponing taxes on capital gains can have a meaningful impact on portfolio growth.

The goal of tax optimization isn’t to avoid paying taxes, but to maximize your wealth on an after-tax basis. One key strategy to achieve this goal is tax deferral.

Unlike mutual funds and exchange-traded funds, which may pass along capital gains to investors annually due to portfolio manager decisions or the trades of other investors, Separately Managed Accounts (SMAs) give investors control over when to realize gains. This flexibility is a key benefit of tax management.

Tax management has two main components: tax-loss harvesting and deferring capital gains. Loss harvesting is straightforward—realized capital losses can offset other realized capital gains, potentially reducing taxable income. For example, assuming there are capital gains to be offset, multiplying your losses by your tax rate can provide an estimated tax savings. On the other hand, deferring capital gains allows your money to stay invested in the market longer, compounding over time. For instance, consider how $50,000 in tax savings could grow over 10 or 20 years, depending on market returns.

The Value of Compounding

Annualized Market Return $50k Tax Savings Invested in the Market Over…
10 Years 20 Years
5% $81,445 $132,665
7% $98,358 $193,484
10% $129,688 $336,375

Source: Neuberger Berman.

It’s important to note that deferring taxes doesn’t mean avoiding them. While some investors may use strategies like passing securities through their estate to benefit from a step-up in cost basis or donating securities to charity, others will eventually pay taxes when withdrawing from their portfolio. However, even with a lower cost basis, a tax-managed portfolio can potentially achieve better after-tax results due to the compounding of market returns on more dollars staying invested rather than being paid to tax. For example, a $1 million portfolio that defers taxes for 30 years can significantly outperform one that pays taxes annually.

Waiting to Pay Taxes Can Boost Asset Growth

Hypothetical $1 Million 30-Year Investment at 10% Annual Return

Source: Neuberger Berman. The 10% annual return roughly corresponds to that of the S&P 500’s total return from 1927 to 2024.

While the concept is simple, deferring gains and realizing losses requires careful monitoring to ensure the portfolio stays aligned with its target benchmark. In short, tax considerations should not overshadow investment objectives. A skilled implementation partner can effectively manage taxes in a portfolio, balancing specific tax goals with minimized impact on portfolio risk.

Takeaways

Tax optimization strategies, particularly systematically deferring capital gains and harvesting capital losses, are critical tools for maximizing after-tax wealth over time. By keeping more of your investment dollars in the market and allowing them to compound, you can unlock significant long-term value. While tax deferral does not eliminate tax obligations, it provides flexibility and the opportunity to manage when and how those taxes are realized. For investors seeking to enhance their portfolio’s efficiency, systematic tax management can be a powerful tool for achieving financial goals.

This material is provided for informational and educational purposes only and nothing herein constitutes investment, legal, accounting or tax advice. This material is general in nature and is not directed to any category of investors and should not be regarded as individualized, a recommendation, investment advice or a suggestion to engage in or refrain from any investment-related course of action. Investment decisions and the appropriateness of this material should be made based on an investor’s individual objectives and circumstances and in consultation with his or her advisors. Information is obtained from sources deemed reliable, but there is no representation or warranty as to its accuracy, completeness or reliability. All information is current as of the date of this material and is subject to change without notice. The firm, its employees and advisory accounts may hold positions of any companies discussed. Any views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of the firm as a whole. Neuberger Berman products and services may not be available in all jurisdictions or to all client types.

This material may include estimates, outlooks, projections and other “forward-looking statements.” Due to a variety of factors, actual events or market behavior may differ significantly from any views expressed.

Investing entails risks, including possible loss of principal. Investments in hedge funds and private equity are speculative and involve a higher degree of risk than more traditional investments. Investments in hedge funds and private equity are intended for sophisticated investors only. Indexes are unmanaged and are not available for direct investment. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

This material is being issued on a limited basis through various global subsidiaries and affiliates of Neuberger Berman Group LLC. Please visit www.nb.com/disclosure-global-communications for the specific entities and jurisdictional limitations and restrictions.

The “Neuberger Berman” name and logo are registered service marks of Neuberger Berman Group LLC.

data.title: Fidelity Small Cap Index Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Small Cap Index Fund is a diversified domestic small-cap equity strategy that seeks to closely track the returns and characteristics of the Russell 2000® Index.
  • The Russell 2000® Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index designed to measure the performance of the smallest 2,000 companies in the Russell 3000® Index.
  • The fund aims to hold each constituent security at approximately the same weight as the index, but may also employ an optimization approach that relies on historical data and correlations to build a portfolio of securities that closely matches the composition and characteristics of the benchmark. As part of this approach, a proxy security may be used in instances where it may be suboptimal or impractical to hold the same security as the index, such as foreign ownership restrictions, round-lot size constraints, compliance limitations, and liquidity and tax issues.

[1] Life of Fund (LOF) if performance is less than 10 years. Fund inception date: 09/08/2011.

[2] This expense ratio is from the most recent prospectus and generally is based on amounts incurred during the most recent fiscal year, or estimated amounts for the current fiscal year in the case of a newly launched fund. It does not include any fee waivers or reimbursements, which would be reflected in the fund's net expense ratio.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate; therefore, you may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares. Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance stated. To learn more or to obtain the most recent month-end performance, visit fidelity.com/performance, institutional.fidelity.com, or 401k.com. Total returns are historical and include change in share value and reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any.

Cumulative total returns are reported as of the period indicated.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

Performance Review

The fund finished the first quarter about in line with the -9.48% result of the Russell 2000® Index. Security misweights and litigation payments from a prior fiscal year aided results this period. The performance variance between futures held by the fund to equitize cash and accruals, and that of the index, had a slightly negative impact to overall performance. Efficient trading and implementation strategies also helped limit fund transaction costs while replicating the exposures and characteristics of the index.

U.S. stocks declined in the first three months of 2025, according to the Russell index, as post-election optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs.

Amid rising volatility, the index posted a notable gain through February 19, but then sharply reversed course and fell solidly into negative territory by the end of the quarter, particularly dragged down by growth stocks and smaller-caps. In January, the Federal Reserve paused following three consecutive rate cuts beginning in September, extending its wait-and-see phase and indicating further reductions would depend on "real progress on inflation" or unexpected weakness in labor. In March, the central bank affirmed this stance at its March 19 meeting, when it held steady its benchmark federal funds rate as it assessed sweeping policy changes and the effect on the economy. Meanwhile, confidence and sentiment among U.S. slipped, mostly reflecting a gloomier outlook for inflation and the economy's path ahead.

Post-election momentum lost steam in mid-February, as the index returned -5.35% for the month. The downtrend steepened in March (-6.81%), when the index slid into a correction.

For the quarter, growth (-11%) shares lagged value (-8%). The index's decline was narrowly concentrated in the growth-oriented information technology (-18%) and consumer discretionary (-15%) sectors, as well as communication services (-13%). These three groups combined represented roughly a quarter of the index for the three months. Elsewhere, energy returned -13%, as the price of oil declined. Industrials returned -11%.

In contrast, utilities stocks rose 5% to lead the way, as investors sought out asset classes with more-defensive qualities. Consumer staples (0%) and real estate (-3%) – both considered more-defensive sectors – also outpaced the Russell index. Financials returned -4%, while materials (-7%) and health care (-8%) also declined.

Regardless of the market environment, we continue to apply a disciplined investment process across all our strategies, relying on highly skilled professionals and robust investment infrastructure. Investment performance is the foundation of our value proposition for shareholders. This is true of our comprehensive suite of low-cost index funds. We expect our index funds to deliver low tracking difference, which is the difference in a fund's performance to that of its stated benchmark. We also seek to minimize tracking error, which measures the volatility of these return differences over a period of time. Whether it's through solid trading techniques for funds that replicate an index or our optimization techniques, when necessary, we are focused on delivering returns in line with benchmark performance.

FUND INFORMATION

Manager(s): Geode Capital Management

Trading Symbol: FSSNX

Start Date: September 08, 2011

Size (in millions): $24,173.13

Morningstar Category: Fund Small Blend

Stock markets, especially foreign markets, are volatile and can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market, or economic developments. Investments in smaller companies may involve greater risks than those in larger, more well-known companies.

Definitions and Important Information

Unless otherwise expressly disclosed to you in writing, the information provided in this material is for educational purposes only. Any viewpoints expressed by Fidelity are not intended to be used as a primary basis for your investment decisions and are based on facts and circumstances at the point in time they are made and are not particular to you.

Accordingly, nothing in this material constitutes impartial investment advice or advice in a fiduciary capacity, as defined or under the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 or the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, both as amended. Fidelity and its representatives may have a conflict of interest in the products or services mentioned in this material because they have a financial interest in the products or services and may receive compensation, directly or indirectly, in connection with the management, distribution, and/or servicing of these products or services, including Fidelity funds, certain third-party funds and products, and certain investment services. Before making any investment decisions, you should take into account all of the particular facts and circumstances of your or your client's individual situation and reach out to a professional adviser, if applicable.

CHARACTERISTICS

Earnings-Per-Share growth Trailing measures the growth in reported earnings per share over trailing one- and five-year periods.

Earnings-Per-Share growth (IBES 1-Year Forecast) measures the growth in reported earnings per share as estimated by Wall Street analysts.

Median Market Cap identifies the median market capitalization of the portfolio or benchmark as determined by the underlying security market caps.

Price-to-Book (P/B) Ratio is the ratio of a company's current share price to reported accumulated profits and capital.

Price/Cash Flow is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months cash flow per share.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio (IBES 1-Year Forecast) is the ratio of a company's current share price to Wall Street analysts' estimates of earnings.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio Trailing is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months earnings per share.

Return on Equity (ROE) 5-Year Trailing is the ratio of a company's last five years historical profitability to its shareholders' equity.

Preferred stock is included as part of each company's net worth.

Sales-Per-Share growth measures the growth in reported sales over the specified past time period.

Weighted Average Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the average equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Weighted Median Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the median equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

INDICES

It is not possible to invest directly in an index. All indices represented are unmanaged. All indices include reinvestment of dividends and interest income unless otherwise noted.

Russell 2000 Index is a market capitalization-weighted index designed to measure the performance of the small-cap segment of the U.S. equity market. It includes approximately 2,000 of the smallest securities in the Russell 3000 Index.

MARKET-SEGMENT WEIGHTS

Market-segment weights illustrate examples of sectors or industries in which the fund may invest, and may not be representative of the fund's current or future investments. They should not be construed or used as a recommendation for any sector or industry.

RANKING INFORMATION

© 2025 Morningstar, Inc. All rights reserved. The Morningstar information contained herein: (1) is proprietary to Morningstar and/or its content providers; (2) may not be copied or redistributed; and (3) is not warranted to be accurate, complete or timely. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Fidelity does not review the Morningstar data and, for mutual fund performance, you should check the fund's current prospectus for the most up-to-date information concerning applicable loads, fees and expenses.

% Rank in Morningstar Category is the fund's total-return percentile rank relative to all funds that have the same Morningstar Category. The highest (or most favorable) percentile rank is 1 and the lowest (or least favorable) percentile rank is 100. The top-performing fund in a category will always receive a rank of 1%. % Rank in Morningstar Category is based on total returns which include reinvested dividends and capital gains, if any, and exclude sales charges.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN STATISTICS

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (less) than 1 is more (less) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

Before investing in any mutual fund, please carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For this and other information, call or write Fidelity for a free prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus. Read it carefully before you invest.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Views expressed are through the end of the period stated and do not necessarily represent the views of Fidelity. Views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and Fidelity disclaims any responsibility to update such views. These views may not be relied on as investment advice and, because investment decisions for a Fidelity fund are based on numerous factors, may not be relied on as an indication of trading intent on behalf of any Fidelity fund. The securities mentioned are not necessarily holdings invested in by the portfolio manager(s) or FMR LLC. References to specific company securities should not be construed as recommendations or investment advice.

Other third-party marks appearing herein are the property of their respective owners.

data.title: HPI: Accumulation Here Could Reward Patient Investors
data.description:
Overview

John Hancock Preferred Income Fund (NYSE:HPI) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total return through its portfolio of income-oriented securities such as preferred stocks, convertible securities, and bonds. The fund is managed by John Hancock and has a public inception dating back to 2002. Since the fund has over two decades worth of operating history, the underlying strategy has proven to be efficient at creating income for shareholders. An income-focused fund like HPI may not be for everyone, so I advise investors to determine what their specific objective or goal is. HPI should ultimately be utilized for income and should not be a part of your portfolio if you are looking to maximize your total return potential.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that HPI's price has fallen by about 3.7%. When including all of the distributions paid out over the same time frame, the total return jumps up to about 4.8%. The whole appeal of a fund like HPI is the combination of capital preservation and consistent income generation. HPI provides a generous starting dividend yield of 9.3%, which makes it an attractive choice for the investors seeking a reliable source of income. As a bonus, distributions are paid out on a monthly basis.

HPI currently trades at a very slight premium to NAV valuation at the moment. I've noticed that the value of HPI has remained suppressed as of 2022, which is coincidentally when interest rates were aggressively hiked by the Fed. The fund has remained resilient through unfavorable macroeconomic conditions, which leads me to believe that accumulation here could be an ideal strategy for patient long term investors. Lower interest rates can serve as a positive catalyst for growth over the next two years. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy and investment approach that HPI implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, HPI has total managed assets over $681M that is spread across approximately 154 different holdings. A large focus on the fund is to generate income from a diverse range of sectors and asset types. Preferred Securities make up the largest bulk of assets at 54.45%. This is followed by exposure to Corporate Bonds and US Government Agency investments, making up 41.59% and 2.22% of assets respectively.

Looking at the largest issuers, we can piece together that many of the preferred securities are exposed to the Financial or Utilities sectors. For instance, Wells Fargo (WFC) is the largest holding at 5.16% of assets. This is followed by varying weights to other large-cap companies like Morgan Stanley (MS), Bank of America (BAC), CMS Energy (CMS), and Vistra Corp (VST).

It's important to understand that HPI has exposure to these holdings through preferred shares, which offers better stability but less capital appreciation compared to common shares. Preferred securities have priority over common stock when it comes to dividend payments, but they are lower than bonds on the payment hierarchy. Preferred securities are essentially a way for businesses to raise capital without giving up control, which is why preferred holders aren't typically given any voter rights. For this reason, HPI is likely to always underperform its own holdings from a price perspective.

Just to demonstrate this, we can see how HPI is outperformed by many of its own holdings. HPI essentially distributes out any of its growth as a dividend so there should be no expectation for any capital appreciation over time. Therefore, most of the value of a fund like this can be derived from its ability to preserve investor capital over the long term.

HPI has an inception dating back to 2002 but the fund never recovered back above the Great Financial Crisis crash in 2008. Therefore, I only pulled the operating data over the last ten year period. Over the last decade, we can see that HPI's price has declined by more than 23.5%. However, you'll notice by looking at the purple line that most of this decline only happened after 2022. This is coincidentally when interest rates were hiked to their decade highs. I anticipate that HPI's price will ultimately recover back to its pre-pandemic levels once interest rates eventually come back down.

Valuation

Since HPI operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, HPI now trades at a slight premium to NAV of about 3%. Looking back over the fund's history, we can see that HPI more commonly traded in the premium territory over the last decade. For reference, HPI has traded at an average premium to NAV of 2.6% over the last three year period. When funds trade at a consistent premium, it's usually a sign of the fund's strength and confidence that the market has in its ability to provide consistent earnings or distributions.

In order to get better clarity on the fund's internal strength, I reviewed the most recent semi-annual report from January of 2025. Net investment income landed at $0.51 per share while net realized gains amounted to $0.39 per share. Looking back on prior years, net investment income seems to be on track to stay aligned with the total for the full year of 2024. However, we can see that net investment income has trended downward from the high of 2020.

On a more positive note, more than half of the fund's distribution is supported by net investment income, which takes away from HPI's reliance on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth. For reference, the NAV remains below in 2020 amount but the sentiment has shifted to a more positive momentum since the lows experienced in 2023. Here are the year-end NAV amounts going back to 2020:

  • 2020: $18.43 per share
  • 2021: $20.81 per share
  • 2022: $17.99 per share
  • 2023: $15.31 per share
  • 2024: $16.13 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $16.29 per share

The reason that HPI's net investment income could be falling is because the majority of the fund has exposure to securities with long term maturities. For instance, over 55.8% of the securities have a maturity date that is over 30 years out. Longer term securities have a higher sensitivity to interest rates and they are essentially trapped at a lower yield, which prevents HPI from rotating its focus onto higher yielding assets.

Risk Profile

When it comes to the risk profile of income funds, a lot of it can be linked to the underlying credit quality of the income investments. Thankfully, part of HPI's strategy puts emphasis on maintaining at least a 50% weight to securities with an investment grade rating. For reference, anything rated BBB and above are considered to be investment grade, while anything rated BB and below is considered to be below investment grade. The breakdown is as follows:

Investment Grade (A - BBB): 57.23%

Below Investment Grade (BB - CCC & Below): 38.24%

As part of the fund's strategy, HPI utilizes leverage equivalent to 37.7% of assets. I consider this to be quite aggressive in this higher interest rate environment. Leverage is typically used in an effort to amplify gains when an attractive opportunity presents itself. However, there's always a risk that leverage can also amplify losses if allocated towards a poor investment. I would ideally like to see leverage decreased over the remainder of 2025 as I believe that leverage usage above 25% is aggressive. Leverage during a period of elevated interest rates can also have a negative impact on operating margins for the fund as a larger portion of its earnings need to be allocated towards debt maintenance.

The biggest risk to a fund with a large exposure to below investment grade securities is the probability of defaults. The higher the interest rates, the higher the cost of debt for borrowers, which typically leads to higher defaults. For instance, S&P Global indicates that leveraged loan defaults could increase to 1.6% by December of 2025, assuming that interest rate cuts take longer than anticipated. Looking at the chart below, we can see how the price of HPI reacted negatively to interest rate increases that took place throughout 2022 and 2023.

The Fed does still anticipate that there will be two small 25-basis-point cuts to take place at some point throughout 2025. However, there are a lot of uncertainties that could cause interest rates to remain unchanged or even increase over the next twelve months. The ripple effects of President Trump's tariff proposals are still to be determined and the probability of a cut is highly dependent on which direction inflationary data goes. Inflation still remains higher than anticipated and if it continues to increase as a result of the tariffs, we could see interest rates increase and further suppress HPI's price.

Questionable Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1235 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9.3%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.482 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, HPI has a mixed history of being able to out earn its distributions. This doesn't instill the most confidence that the distribution can be sustained through continued headwinds. Just for reference, here are the annual earning amounts over the last several years:

  • 2020: ($1.46) per share
  • 2021: $3.86 per share
  • 2022: ($1.34) per share
  • 2023: ($1.20) per share
  • 2024: $2.30 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.90 per share

As you can see, HPI has only been able to out earn its distribution for two of the last five years. As of the first half of 2025, HPI is on track to cover the distribution but we will have to see the next annual report after July ends to confirm. We can see that the distribution was reduced before 2020 and has never been increased again since then, even when HPI had strong earnings in 2021. Therefore, I wouldn't be surprised if the distribution is cut once more at some point if interest rates remain high and suppress the earnings potential of the fund.

Additionally, the distributions seem to be comprised of a combination between net investment income and return of capital. The fund has historically leaned on return of capital to help make up some of the underperformance. I normally wouldn't have an issue with the usage of return of capital but since the NAV remains below its pre-pandemic level and the distribution coverage is a bit mixed, I believe that this reliance on return of capital could be harmful.

Additionally, the distributions funded by net investment income are also classified as ordinary dividend income. Ordinary dividends have less favorable tax consequences that investors must account for. Based on your current tax bracket, amount of capital invested, and desired income level, a position in HPI may be best utilized within a tax advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am rating HPI as a hold for now. On one hand, the valuation could be attractive and patient investors may benefit from accumulation here in anticipation of future interest rate cuts. On the other hand, the dividend sustainability is questionable and if interest rates remain high, HPI's valuation and dividend coverage could remain suppressed. aThere's also the chance of higher interest rates having a negative effect on the leverage that HPI deploys, as well as the possibility for defaults within some of its below investment rated securities.

data.title: Ford: Suspending Guidance Is Not The Only Issue For Shareholders
data.description:
Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) just released its Q1 2025 results and although the market is reacting negatively, it seems that the bad news were mostly priced-in already.

The good news was that both Non-GAAP earnings and revenue came above the consensus estimates, but suspending the guidance on the back of significant uncertainty through the rest of 2025 was more than enough to offset that.

The company has suspended full-year 2025 guidance due to tariff-related uncertainties and potential industry-wide supply chain disruptions.

Source: Seeking Alpha

So far the anticipated net adverse impact on the company's operating profit is expected to be around $1.5bn, but given the complexity of industry supply chains, the probability of further escalation of the trade war and the collapsing consumer confidence this estimate could easily exceed current expectations.

As usual, when adverse events like this happen, investors resort to the extremely low earnings multiple, which on a Non-GAAP forward basis is once again in single digits. The uncertainty around 2025 profitability, however, has now once again made this multiple relatively meaningless.

What Was Q1 2025 All About?

As a matter of fact, the aforementioned $1.5bn adverse impact is solely based on tariffs and does not seem to factor in a potential economic slowdown and drop in consumer spending. Moreover, the $1.5bn figure is a net number, which includes roughly $1bn of assumed offsetting factors.

Based on what we know now, our expectations of how certain details will resolve around tariffs, we've estimated the gross impact of tariffs for full year total company EBIT of $2.5 billion and a net impact of $1.5 billion.

Source: Ford Q1 2025 Earnings Transcript

I would not spend too much time speculating on these numbers, but the below extract from the earnings release speaks for itself. It seems that these $1bn worth of offsets are highly speculative and based on pricing, volumes and competitive dynamics - all factors that could change dramatically in such an environment.

So the largest element of the offset is market equation optimization (...)

(...) we do have some cost mitigation examples in there as well, such as we said in our prepared remarks, vehicles shipped to Canada from Mexico via the US are now transported on bonded carriers, so they aren't subject to US tariffs, and we're doing similar actions on parts as well.

(...) to come up with the $1 billion, we ran a range of market factor scenarios, segment-by-segment, channel-by-channel, vehicle-by-vehicle, inclusive of a competitive landscape. We varied inputs such as pricing, such as volumes, such as SAR and we considered that there are certain segments, for instance, where we have 100% of production in the US like our full-size pickup trucks, where competitors are much less than that. So we've taken all of these competitive dynamics as well as these inputs into consideration and have ran a number of permutations. When we triangulate on that, it comes up with this $1 billion, inclusive of some of the cost items that I mentioned.

Source: Ford Q1 2025 Earnings Transcript

Automotive Struggles

Another major development during the quarter was the fact that Ford's automotive segment (including all segments, except for Ford Credit) is once again loss making on an operating income basis. By excluding Ford Credit's revenue and interest, operating and other expenses (marked in blue below), we could estimate that the EBIT margin of the Automotive segment now in negative territory - a drastic change from the 2.6% margin in Q1 of 2024.

On a long-term basis, Ford's price/sales multiple is strongly related to these automotive margins and at first it appears that the current sales multiple is mostly in-line with profitability on a trailing-twelve month basis.

Of course, the problem is that the figures above are based on 9-months' worth of data from 2024 and as we have seen above - the rest of 2025 is likely to be materially different from the comparable period of 2024.

Ford's newly introduced business segments also seem to rely heavily on the so-called Ford Pro, which is a segment for commercial, government, and rental customers.

During the quarter, Ford Pro segment was the only one where the company is still achieving meaningful operating profits. The internal combustion engine segment for retail customers - Ford Blue is now barely making a profit, while Model e Segment is still deeply into negative territory.

The Model e Segment is unlikely to become profitable anytime soon as it would require significant economies of scale - something that is not yet feasible based on the segment's relatively small size. Ford Blue's revenues have also been relatively flat in 2024 and the 3% decline in Q1 2025 from the prior year is foreshadowing a tough year ahead.

This leaves, Ford Pro segment as the only bright spot within the automotive business. So far the segment is resilient on the back of strong commercial and government spending, but in the case of a recession this could change rather quickly.

A Bright Spot (So Far)

In addition to Ford Pro, Ford Credit is the other area of the company that is not yet feeling the full pressure of tariffs and a potential economic slowdown.

Since the bottom in 2023, operating profit within the segment has been gradually improving. This has come on the back of strong financing margins as interest rates normalize and high auction values.

However, it appears that investors should not extrapolate this through the rest of 2025 as Ford's management has already warned analysts during the Q1 2025 call that the tailwind might dissipate in the second half of the year.

We've seen elevated auction prices already and that's reflecting obviously used across the industry, relatively low used vehicle and stock. And we're thinking that with the higher new vehicle prices as a result of tariffs, we think that the auction values -- that could lend support to auction values in the near-term, but the -- that might be muted somewhat by a slowdown in the economy in the second half.

Source: Ford Q1 2025 Earnings Transcript

During the quarter we also saw loss-to-receivables ratio jumping notably from a year ago and that's something that investors should pay close attention to during the next earnings release.

Overall, lending conditions in the automotive sector have been easing over the past 2-year period and this now holds risk of credit losses for those who have become irresponsible in their lending standards.

Lastly, the second quarter of FY 2025 is highly likely to be a good one for Ford Credit as used vehicle prices continue to improve.

The SUV sub-segment noted the highest increase in April which is good news for the likes of Ford and General Motors (GM), which rely heavily on their pricing power in the segment.

Conclusion

Although bad news were mostly priced-in already, Ford's Q1 2025 earnings release was a major red flag for investors. In addition to the significant uncertainty through the rest of 2025, Ford's results could suffer meaningfully as some ongoing tailwind could disappear in the second half of the year. The low valuation multiples are unlikely to save investors from underperforming the market and with that I would abstain from rushing-in to "buy the dip".

data.title: Upwork: With Near Zero Growth, It's Time To Sell The Post-Earnings Spike (Downgrade)
data.description:
The stock market is very jittery as we move throughout the Q1 earnings season, and naturally investors are on the lookout for companies to revise their outlooks downward as companies digest the impact of tariffs and a slowing macroeconomy on their businesses.

Upwork (NASDAQ:UPWK), meanwhile, has managed to do the opposite: to raise its outlook, as least on the bottom line, amid a difficult climate. Shares of Upwork rallied more than 20% in the immediate aftermath of its Q1 earnings release and guidance boost, reversing all of its YTD losses and bringing the stock up nearly 30% over a one-year timeframe. The critical question for investors now is: is this the beginning of a longer rally for Upwork, or is this just a one-time spike?

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Upwork in February, when the stock was trading at similar ~$16 levels. Since then, in my view, the outlook has only darkened for the company. Businesses are cutting hiring and discretionary spending, and are deploying AI wherever possible to eliminate expensive contract labor - which is exactly where Upwork thrives. Potentially showcasing some of these trends, Upwork has decelerated to near-zero growth, and despite the boost to adjusted EBITDA expectations, I believe the long-term risks here outweigh the cheap entry prices. As such, I think the post-earnings rally is a great time to lock in any gains on this stock, and I'm downgrading Upwork to a sell rating.

To me, these are the core red flags that make me extremely wary on Upwork:

  • Upwork's revenue growth is slowing to near-zero, while active clients are declining. Cost-cutting has become the predominant attitude in the current macro slowdown, with executives responding to growing uncertainties by slashing controllable costs. Amid this push for efficiency, Upwork will lose more corporate mandates for contract work - and this decline is reflected in the company's drop in active clients.
  • Long-term AI risk. Upwork continues to tout its progress in AI, noting in the most recent quarter that AI-related GSV is up 25% y/y. Yet in the long term, many of the core functions that Upwork contractors can perform are big candidates for AI automation.
  • Competition. Upwork is far from the only freelancer marketplace in business: it's also facing direct competition from the likes of Fiverr (FVRR). Upwork may see its take rates compress as it fights for a shrinking pie against its rivals.

Even after the post-earnings rally, Upwork remains a value stock especially against its raised guidance: but in my view, its cheapness doesn't compensate investors enough for its myriad risks. At current share prices near $16, Upwork trades at a market cap of $2.16 billion. After we net off the $622.0 million of cash and $358.4 million of debt on Upwork's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $1.90 billion.

Meanwhile, for the remainder of FY25, Upwork has retained its revenue outlook of $740-$760 million (-1% to -4%), while indicating that adjusted EBITDA margins will be better: with a new range of $190-$200 million representing a 26% margin at the midpoint, versus $180-$190 million (25% margin) previously. It's also boosting its pro forma EPS guidance to $1.14-$1.18, the midpoint of which is a 7% increase from its prior expectations of $1.05-$1.10.

This puts Upwork's valuation at:

  • 9.7x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA
  • 13.8x FY25 P/E

To me, a cheap valuation is the only "upside risk" I see in Upwork. However, I take very little comfort in the company's tepid growth rates, its long-term incompatibility with AI and automation, and its client churn.

Take advantage of the post-earnings spike to sell, and reinvest the proceeds elsewhere.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Upwork's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Upwork's revenue grew only 1% y/y to $192.7 million, slightly beating Wall Street's expectations of $188.5 million (-1% y/y), but also decelerating three points from a 4% y/y growth rate in Q4. Meanwhile, we note that GSV is declining: down -2% y/y to $987.7 million. GSV, or gross services value, measures the total dollar value of the services transacted on Upwork's marketplace platform, and the decline is a telling signal of the less-enthusiastic enterprise climate. Moreover, we note that the number of of active clients on Upwork's platform also fell -7% y/y to 812k:

Upwork continues to tout its progress in AI. The company notes that overall AI-related GSV grew 25% y/y, while GSV from prompt engineering tasks grew 52% y/y. The company also notes that hourly rates from its freelancers doing AI tasks are at a 40% premium compared to everyone else on its platform.

Clearly, however, progress on AI needs to be measured on a net basis - certainly the company is enjoying growth from AI-specific task requests, but it's also presumably losing a lot of business from clients automating what was once done by a freelance contractor. Upwork's tepid 1% y/y revenue growth and its declining customer counts and GSV offer us a telling picture that the net impact of AI, on top of a tough macroeconomy, is likely negative for Upwork and will continue to be so for the long haul.

From a margin perspective, Upwork grew its adjusted EBITDA by 68% y/y to $56.0 million, hitting a 29% adjusted EBITDA margin that improved 12 points y/y.

However, the company warned that Q1 would be the "high water-mark" for margins for the rest of the year, as it plans to invest aggressively in the back half of FY25 to try to reinvigorate growth. Per CFO Erica Gessert's remarks on the Q&A portion of the Q1 earnings call:

So, yes, sales and marketing was at an all-time low in Q1. We talked a lot in Q4 about the optimization of enterprise business, about improving those costs to serve and costs to acquire through the Business Plus strategy. And so you are seeing that reflected in our numbers. But we're also very clear, like you said, that Q1 would be the high watermark for margins for this year. And so, we do intend to reinvest. We see ample opportunities for growth in this business. Hayden talked on the prepared remarks about Enterprise -- this being a transitional period for Enterprise and we do intend to reinvest there to unlock some of the contingent labor TAM that we've been talking about. As well as on the R&D side, continued investment in the AI enablement of the platform. So you will see those investments come through in Q2 and Q3 this year. But I think we've shown over time that we can both invest in growth and create meaningful margin expansion, so we feel really good about both."

At this point, robust margin expansion and a lifted adjusted EBITDA guidance are all that Upwork has going for it: so I fear that sentiment in this stock may weaken as we move throughout the year and Upwork comes off of its 29% margins from Q1.

Key takeaways

To me, Upwork's positioning as a value stock (even after its sharp post-earnings rally) reflects its tremendous risks, both in the short term and the long run. In the short term, the company faces a weakening macroeconomy that has already driven GSV and active client declines, on top of a plan to reinvest margin gains back into growth - which may not yield positive results. In the long run, the market for freelance work may dry up with the increased proliferation of AI tools.

Lock in gains and sell here.

data.title: Roku Q1 2025 Earnings: Growth Slows, But Monetization And Margins Improve
data.description:
Takeaways from the Roku’s 1Q25 earnings release

Yes, Roku’s revenue and EBITDA growth slowed in Q1 2025 - and it’s not just a seasonal blip. Revenue rose 15.8% year-over-year, down from 22.0% in Q4. EBITDA growth also decelerated, landing at 36.9% compared to 62.5% the previous quarter. The slowdown is real, and it’s raising legitimate questions about how well Roku’s monetization strategy is holding up now that scale isn’t the main constraint anymore.

So what does this actually tell us? For starters, engagement still isn’t translating cleanly into revenue. In Q4, Total Reach Campaign (TRC) usage was up 82%, but that surge hasn’t been mirrored in top-line growth - a gap that continued into Q1. That suggests ad yield and efficiency aren’t fully dialed in yet. Roku may be brushing up against short-term ceilings in either ad load or CPMs. It also hints that platform leverage - turning user growth into consistent profitability -may take longer to materialize than investors hoped.

Recalibrating Thesis for Roku

But it’s important to separate signal from noise. This doesn’t necessarily mean the model is broken. Margins are still expanding, just at a slower pace. It also doesn’t mean Roku can’t reaccelerate. The upcoming quarters, with tailwinds like the Olympics and back-to-school ad cycles, could provide a better environment. And from a valuation perspective, the stock doesn’t look overhyped - trading at around 2x sales with positive free cash flow, it’s no longer priced like a high-flyer.

The real shift here is in how Roku should be framed. It’s moving out of “growth stock” territory and into “compounder candidate” territory. The key question now is whether Roku can grow EBITDA and free cash flow by 20–30% annually, even if revenue growth stays in the 12–15% range. If the answer is yes, then the stock remains undervalued — but you’re betting on margin expansion and operating leverage, not explosive revenue gains.

Looking ahead, there are a few markers to watch. The bull case strengthens if EBITDA margins continue climbing toward (or past) 10%, ARPU keeps growing in the double digits, and Roku begins closing the gap between TRC engagement and monetization. You’d also want to see small-business adoption and demand-side platform (DSP) integrations driving more ad demand. On the flip side, it’s a warning sign if revenue growth dips below 12%, quarterly EBITDA stalls under $60–70 million, free cash flow turns structurally negative, or engagement starts plateauing - especially on the TRC or home screen.

At the end of the day, the market’s asking a simple but critical question: Can Roku actually monetize like a platform — not just look like one? If the answer’s yes, this quarter is just a breather before the next leg up. If not, the stock could stay in “show-me” mode for a while.

Peer Comparison for Roku

Shifting gears, let’s try to compare the company’s financials against a set of peers. However, the company doesn’t sit neatly in one category. It’s part CTV infrastructure, part ad tech, part streaming service, and part consumer platform. That kind of overlap makes it tough to find a single, clean comparison. So the smarter move is to build a blended peer group, organized by how each company makes money and what strategic role they play.

Let’s break it down by category:

1. Streaming Platforms (SVOD/AVOD hybrids)

These are companies running both subscription and ad-supported streaming models — similar to Roku Channel’s hybrid approach.

Netflix (NFLX): Their recent ad-tier rollout mirrors Roku’s model, and they bring scale and original content to the table.

Disney (DIS): With both Hulu and Disney+ under its belt, Disney combines subscriptions with ad-supported streaming across a massive content portfolio.

Paramount Global (PARA): Owns Pluto TV, one of the bigger FAST (free ad-supported streaming TV) platforms - a direct comp to Roku Channel.

2. FAST & Ad-Supported Streamers

These are Roku Channel’s most direct competitors in the free, ad-supported space.

Fox Corp (FOXA): Owns Tubi, a pure-play FAST service gaining traction in CTV.

Comcast (CMCSA): Runs Peacock (which mixes SVOD and AVOD) and has a growing presence in smart TV operating systems.

Amazon (AMZN): Fire TV competes with Roku hardware and OS, plus it’s layering in more ad-supported content within Prime Video.

3. Ad Tech Platforms (especially in CTV)

Roku makes a big chunk of its money through ads — both programmatic and direct. So comparing it to pure-play ad tech firms is useful.

The Trade Desk (TTD): The closest comp in terms of ad monetization and targeting in CTV.

4. Platform + Hardware Ecosystem Players

Roku’s model is also tied to its OS and hardware - so you can’t ignore these players.

Apple (AAPL): Apple TV combines OS, hardware, and monetization, just like Roku.

Google (GOOGL): Android TV/Google TV competes directly with Roku’s OS, and YouTube is pushing further into ad-supported streaming.

Looking at trailing twelve-month (TTM) revenue growth, Roku stands out at the top of the pack with 13.5% growth — ahead of The Trade Desk at 12.4%, Netflix at 10.7%, and well above Alphabet and Amazon, which both came in below 10%. Traditional media players like Disney, Comcast, and Fox are further behind, with most under 9% and Comcast barely scraping past 2%.

The key takeaway here is that Roku is growing faster than both old-school media companies and many tech peers, yet it trades at a fraction of their valuations. The contrast is especially sharp when compared to The Trade Desk - Roku is growing slightly faster, yet trades at under 2x price-to-sales, while TTD sits around 9.5x. That valuation gap underscores the broader point: Roku is executing more like a premium ad-tech platform than a traditional media or device company. The market, it seems, still hasn’t caught up.

The EBITDA growth story is even more compelling. In the latest quarterly numbers, Roku posted a 109.4% year-over-year increase in EBITDA — the highest among all major peers. The Trade Desk followed at 89.8%, with Google and Amazon at 47.8% and 30.9% respectively. Netflix and Apple posted growth in the low double digits, while Disney, Fox, and Comcast hovered around 13%, with Comcast actually in negative territory. Roku’s operating leverage is accelerating faster than any of its comps, which speaks volumes given the ongoing concerns some investors have had about margins. These results show that Roku’s platform monetization is improving meaningfully, and the company is managing costs with increasing discipline. It’s a clear signal that the business has moved well beyond its hardware roots.

Altogether, these performance metrics validate the core investment thesis: Roku isn’t just recovering — it’s outperforming. The company is growing faster than Netflix and Disney, while scaling operating profit faster than The Trade Desk, yet it remains priced like a legacy media device maker. Roku is being misclassified in the market, and that disconnect is precisely where the opportunity lies. If you’re building a forward-looking valuation case, this is where to focus.

What to watch for in ROKU

To keep a close eye on the bear case for Roku and spot any early cracks in the thesis, it’s smart to focus on the metrics that really matter: how well the platform is monetizing, the health and engagement of the user base, and whether margins are scaling with revenue. These three pillars - monetization efficiency, user quality, and operating leverage - are the foundation of the long thesis. If any of them start to weaken, it could be a sign the story is changing.

Wrap up

Roku’s core investment thesis is still intact, though there are early signs that the company is shifting from a hypergrowth story to one of a scaling, maturing platform. The company continues to execute well — expanding monetization across its platform, deepening its hold as the dominant TV operating system in the U.S., and showing meaningful improvements in operating leverage. The growth is real and diversified, but it’s becoming more measured and execution-driven. We’re moving into a phase where success depends less on sheer user growth and more on how well Roku can optimize each piece of its ecosystem.

The key risk — and it hasn’t changed — is the monetization vs. engagement gap. Roku continues to grow user numbers and watch time, but unless that engagement translates into higher ARPU, the rerating case weakens. This remains the single biggest variable in the bull case. The next few quarters need to show progress in bridging that gap, whether through new ad formats, deeper Frndly integration, or more effective home screen monetization.

So here’s the updated framing: Roku is no longer just an ad-tech story in the making — it’s now a scaled, profitable CTV platform. But to earn a higher multiple, it has to prove it can convert that scale into consistently stronger per-user economics. Until then, it’s best thought of as a “compounder in training” — one with strong optionality, a defensible infrastructure advantage, and a platform that’s still maturing into its full monetization potential.

Disclaimer: This text expresses the views of the author as of the date indicated and such views are subject to change without notice. The author has no duty or obligation to update the information contained herein. Further, wherever there is the potential for profit there is also the possibility of loss. Additionally, the present article is being made available for educational purposes only and should not be used for any other purpose. The information contained herein does not constitute and should not be construed as an offering of advisory services or an offer to sell or solicitation to buy any securities or related financial instruments in any jurisdiction. Some information and data contained herein concerning economic trends and performance is based on or derived from information provided by independent third-party sources. The author trusts that the sources from which such information has been obtained are reliable; however, it cannot guarantee the accuracy of such information and has not independently verified the accuracy or completeness of such information or the assumptions on which such information is based.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Novo Nordisk Q1 2025: One Of The Best Deals You Can Get Right Now
data.description:
My last article on Novo Nordisk (NVO) was about three months ago, and I considered the company to be one of the best opportunities on the market. Since then, only one thing has changed: NVO has fallen by a further 20% and is now a strong buy that I have little doubt about. Clearly, no one can be certain of making a good investment, but this company's figures are exceptional, and I am perplexed when I look at the price chart.

A never-ending decline from its all-time high, the largest the company has experienced since the 1990s. Yet, Q1 2025 results prove once again that this company is far from dead.

Q1 2025 Highlights

When a company's value drops by 55%, you would expect it to be losing money or have a tiny growth rate, but this is not the case with NVO.

Sales reached DKK 78.08 billion, an increase of 19% compared to Q1 2024; operating income grew by 20% and net profit by 14%. We are beyond a simple double-digit figure for a giant that has dominated the obesity market for decades.

For 2025, management expects CER revenue growth of between 13% and 21%, lower than the 16%-24% guidance given in February. Operating income is expected to grow between 16% and 24%, compared to 19% to 27% a few months ago. Clearly, this is not positive news, but let me explain why I am not at all concerned.

The reason for the downward revision of the guidance was due to a slowdown in the growth of its main drugs in the United States. Due to a severe shortage of these drugs, the FDA has allowed US compounding pharmacies to produce legal copies of Wegovy and Novo's diabetes/obesity drug Ozempic. These drugs are vital for treating type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular problems, and obesity more generally, which is why this decision was necessary: people needed to continue their treatment. The point is that in February 2025, the FDA declared that the shortage was over and ordered compounding pharmacies to stop sales by May 22, 2025. Consequently, in a few weeks, the burden on Wegovy and Ozempic sales will inevitably ease if the companies want to avoid legal problems:

Given the significant growth of compounding in the U.S., it appears to impact Wegovy prescription uptake in 2025 so far. We are also taking legal actions against these compounders because we believe they are violating our IP [intellectual property] and, as such, damaging our business.

CEO Lars Fruergaard Jørgensen

At this point, you might think that Wegovy and Ozempic have stopped growing due to competition on the market: nothing could be further from the truth.

Ozempic grew by 18% while Wegovy grew by 85% compared to Q1 2024. It is mind-blowing to think that Novo Nordisk's current share price is close to the same level as in 2021 when Wegovy was just released. In the current valuation, the market has forgotten that NVO has a drug in its portfolio that is growing at almost triple digits and generates $2.60 billion in quarterly sales.

On top of that, the other segments continue to grow, with Rare Disease and Total Insulin up 5% and 4%, respectively. The latter has been a source of recurring revenue for decades, as people with diabetes cannot do without insulin.

NVO has a dominant market share in this industry, controlling 43.50% of global sales volume. Between 150 and 200 million people worldwide need insulin, and what surprises me most is that only half of them have access to it. This market may seem saturated, but I don't think that's really the case: it's essential that everyone has access to insulin. It's not just a question of revenue for NVO.

As for Ozempic and Wegovy, here too, the market share situation is impressive.

Rybelsus®, Ozempic®, and Victoza® have a global market share of 54% in the GLP-1 market. Some may think this is a negative result given that last year it was 55.30%, but perhaps they do not realize how large this market is. UBS estimates sales of $126 billion in 2029, a 2023-2029 sales CAGR of 30%. Having a market share of over 50% in this market is equivalent to having a systematic increase in sales of at least 15-20% per year. For the Obesity Care segment, it is even better, with a volume market share of 68.70% thanks to Wegovy.

Novo Nordisk remains the undisputed leader in everything related to obesity and diabetes. Competition is fierce, but I don't think the market share has fallen so sharply as to justify such a collapse. We are talking about a few percentage points in a market that is set to explode.

Unfortunately, obesity is a growing problem, and more and more people will need these drugs. Since 1990, obesity in adults has doubled; in adolescents, it has quadrupled. In 2022, approximately 2.5 billion people were overweight, and this number is set to rise. Consider that Novo Nordisk is the company with the highest market share in the world in a market that will have billions and billions of people in a few decades. It is absurd, in my opinion, that the market does not realize this and believes that NVO's dominance is declining just because its market share has fallen by 1-2% in a particular segment.

Consider that NVO's products are in such high demand that the company cannot keep up with demand, which is why it has increased its plant CapEx to DKK 13.40 billion in 2025, a 57% increase over last year, and we are only talking about one quarter. The need to meet high demand is such that the buyback plan has been suspended: every penny must be invested to take advantage of the strong, growing demand.

NVO's pipeline is ready to deliver new successful drugs such as CagriSema, which has demonstrated 15.70% greater weight loss in adults with obesity or overweight and type 2 diabetes. The company plans to submit its first application for authorization in Q1 2026. Meanwhile, the oral drug semaglutide 25 mg for obesity has been submitted to the US FDA for review.

The market is concerned about whether CagriSema results in 1% or 2% less weight loss, but the bigger picture is being missed. The numbers show that Novo Nordisk is the leader in a market that could grow by as much as 30% per year and has the potential to replace bariatric surgery in the very long term. Novo Nordisk has been spending billions for decades to be the leader in this industry, and the results are clearly visible. Even if it loses some market share, it's not the end of the world: this market is big enough for everyone. Obesity is not just a problem in the US, but is spreading worldwide due to poor health habits.

Novo Nordisk's sales are growing even faster in emerging markets, China and APAC. But there's more.

Operating income is growing faster than revenue thanks to economies of scale for its main drugs.

So not only is NVO increasing its revenues by 20% per year, but its operating income is growing even faster, and its current level of profitability is among the highest in the entire pharmaceutical industry. All this can be yours for a forward P/E ratio of just 16.35x (perhaps a little more after today).

Over the last few months, I have personally invested heavily in this company, even though I haven't written any new articles. Novo Nordisk has become one of my top holdings in my portfolio, and my latest purchase was made at $57 per share.

As I write this article, I notice that it is up 8% in pre-market trading, and paradoxically, I am not very enthusiastic. I was ready to go all-in at $45-50 per share, but at this point, I don't know if it will ever reach those levels: sooner or later, the market will realize that it is making a miscalculation, and this could have been the turning point. Clearly, anything can happen, and no one has a crystal ball, but I believe a company of this kind deserves a PE ratio FWD of at least 25x, a level still far off despite the recent rise. So, for me, Novo Nordisk remains a strong buy.
